title: Bidirectional Language Models Are Also Few-shot Learners

structured.summary: This paper proposes Sequential Autoregressive Prompting (SAP), an iterative prompting technique that enables bidirectional encoder-decoder models like mT5 to perform few-shot and zero-shot generation tasks without fine-tuning. SAP feeds back only the first generated token at each iteration, effectively converting denoising-pretrained bidirectional models into autoregressive generators, achieving competitive or superior performance to larger unidirectional models on machine translation, question answering, and summarization.

strengths.point: Innovative iterative prompting method tailored for bidirectional models
strengths.evidence: SAP enables bidirectional models pretrained with denoising objectives to perform autoregressive generation by feeding back only the first generated token per iteration, overcoming traditional limitations.

strengths.point: Strong empirical results across diverse NLP tasks
strengths.evidence: SAP with mT5 (3.7B) outperforms larger unidirectional models (GPT-3 6.7B, XGLM 7.5B) on FLORES-101 MT in zero- and few-shot settings, and improves performance on question answering (XQuAD, SQuAD) and summarization (CNN/Daily Mail).

weaknesses.point: Insufficient comparison and positioning relative to prior iterative or incremental decoding methods
weaknesses.evidence: The paper lacks literature review or empirical baselines comparing SAP to existing encoder-decoder incremental decoding techniques such as prefix-tuning or constrained decoding.

weaknesses.point: Lack of detailed ablations and analyses on key SAP design choices and decoding behavior
weaknesses.evidence: No quantitative ablation on feeding back multiple tokens versus single token; no per-iteration quality or error accumulation analysis; unclear decoding strategies; and no detailed stop token handling described.

questions.question: How does SAP performance and efficiency scale with model size and pretraining objectives?
questions.motivation: Understanding SAP's dependence on bidirectional model scale and pretraining would clarify its general applicability and inform future model design.

questions.question: Can SAP be effectively applied or adapted to tasks requiring structured or long-form generation, and how stable is generation over long sequences?
questions.motivation: Evaluating SAP on structured outputs and longer sequences would test its robustness and practical utility beyond the tested tasks.

critique_points.point_001: Insufficient comparison and positioning relative to prior iterative or incremental decoding methods. The paper lacks literature review or empirical baselines comparing SAP to existing encoder-decoder incremental decoding techniques such as prefix-tuning or constrained decoding
critique_points.point_002: Lack of detailed ablations and analyses on key SAP design choices and decoding behavior. No quantitative ablation on feeding back multiple tokens versus single token; no per-iteration quality or error accumulation analysis; unclear decoding strategies; and no detailed stop token handling described
